coredata.dc:title: Regulating π-π interactions in carbon dots via N-doping & ionic liquid modification for enhanced efficiency in 1 M HCl

Hu, Songqing
coredata.dc:description: In this study, we developed ionic liquid-modified nitrogen-doped carbon dots (IL-NCDs) with enhanced π-π interactions, effectively addressing the aggregation issue of conventional carbon dots in strong acidic conditions. The synthesized IL-NCDs demonstrated superior dispersibility and long-term protective performance in 1 M HCl, achieving a corrosion inhibition efficiency of 97.34 % at 60 mg/L and maintaining protection for over 20 days. The optimized surface charge and increased active sites facilitated the formation of a dense protective film, offering an efficient and eco-friendly solution for metal corrosion protection in acidic environments, with significant potential for industrial applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Acidizing inhibitor
Carbon dots
Long-term inhibition
π-π interactions
Electrophilic nucleophilic activity

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of ionic liquid-modified carbon dots (IL-NCD)
Electrochemical study of corrosion inhibition performance and mechanism
Molecular activity analysis of NCD and IL-NCD
Mechanism of action of NCD and IL-NCD as corrosion inhibitors

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images and particle size distributions of NCD (a) and IL-NCD (b), Infrared Spectra (c) and Ultraviolet Spectra (d) of NCD and IL-NCD, XPS peaks of total (e), N 1 s (f), O 1 s (g), and C 1 s (h) of NCD and IL-NCD.
Nyquist, Bode diagrams and Polarization curves of N80 steel in 1 M HCl solution with addition of different concentrations of NCD (a-c) and IL-NCD (d-f).

(a) Corrosion rate of N80 after adding inhibitors of different concentrations, and (b) inhibition efficiency; (c) corrosion rate and (d) inhibition efficiency over time after adding 50 mg/L of inhibitor; the adsorption isotherms of (e) and (f) the two inhibitors.

Morphology of corrosion products on N80 steel in hydrochloric acid (a), comparative morphology of corrosion products/film layers treated with NCD and IL-NCD inhibitors (b, c), XPS full-spectrum analysis (d) and elemental depth profiling: characteristic peaks of Fe 2p (e), in the blank group, additional N 1 s, C 1 s, O 1 s peak in IL-NCD (f-h), accompanied by Zeta potential assessment of two types of carbon dots under acidic conditions (i).
AFM morphology (a) and lateral height profile (b) and Ra value of the metal surface after removal of corrosion products, SEM morphology (d), and EDS elemental content spectrum (e).

Electrostatic potential analysis and extremum (a), average local ionization energy (ALIE) distribution and extremum (b), local electron attachment energy (LEAE) distribution and extremum (c), van der Waals potential analysis with Fe atom (d), Extreme number of electrostatic potential (e), Average ALIE value (f) and Average LEAE value (g) of carbon dots.

AIM (Atoms-In-Molecules) path and electron density (a), and LOL-pi (localized orbital positioning function) isograms (b) of carbon dots, Variation of carbon dots dimer with distance van der Waals force (c).
Mechanism of action of carbon dots as corrosion inhibitors.
The preparation of IL-NCD with edge-charged strong adsorptive sites.

head.ce:title: Regulating π-π interactions in carbon dots via N-doping & ionic liquid modification for enhanced efficiency in 1 M HCl

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: China National Petroleum Corporation, Beijing 100007, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Exploration and Development Research Institute of HuaBei Oilfield Company, Hebei 062552, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Exploration and Development Research Institute of HuaBei Oilfield Company

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Exploration and Development Research Institute of HuaBei Oilfield Company, Hebei 062552, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, we developed ionic liquid-modified nitrogen-doped carbon dots (IL-NCDs) with enhanced π-π interactions, effectively addressing the aggregation issue of conventional carbon dots in strong acidic conditions. The synthesized IL-NCDs demonstrated superior dispersibility and long-term protective performance in 1 M HCl, achieving a corrosion inhibition efficiency of 97.34 % at 60 mg/L and maintaining protection for over 20 days. The optimized surface charge and increased active sites facilitated the formation of a dense protective film, offering an efficient and eco-friendly solution for metal corrosion protection in acidic environments, with significant potential for industrial applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In the field of materials science, metals are widely used due to their excellent mechanical properties, but they are susceptible to environmental corrosion. Hydrochloric acid, as a powerful cleaning agent, increases the risk of metal corrosion. To monitor metal dissolution and acid consumption, the use of corrosion inhibitors containing N, S and O heteroatoms is crucial. These inhibitors effectively slow down the corrosion process through complex chemical interactions, thereby extending the service life of metals [1–4]. In recent years, remarkable progress has been made in the research of carbon dots (CDs) as corrosion inhibitors in the field of corrosion protection of metallic materials [5,6]. Their unique physicochemical properties, including excellent water solubility, superior biocompatibility, and low toxicity, have distinguished CDs as environmentally friendly chemicals, demonstrating remarkable corrosion inhibition capability [7,8]. N-doped CDs (NCD), as the star material among various reported CDs, have been proven to be effective in resisting steel corrosion in harsh environments (e.g., strong acidic), significantly extending the service life of metallic materials [9–11]. In pursuit of better anti-corrosion effect, researchers have delved deep into the application potential of CDs co-doped with other heteroatoms, such as cerium [12,13] and sulfur [14,15]. Systematic evaluations have been carried out to reveal the corrosion inhibition effect of these composite CDs on various metallic materials. By adjusting the types and proportions of doping elements, not only has the corrosion inhibition performance of CDs been enhanced, but their application scope been broadened, offering more comprehensive and efficient anti-corrosion solutions for a wide range of metals [15,16]. However, a fatal weakness of CDs in corrosion protection is their tendency to agglomerate at high concentrations, and their long-term corrosion inhibition performance is unsatisfactory due to weak interactions. Therefore, addressing the agglomeration issue of CDs during the corrosion inhibition process is of great significance, yet it remains a challenge. To reduce the agglomeration of CDs and enhance its adsorption and corrosion inhibition properties, many scholars have focused on the surface chemical modification of CDs. Ye et al. used imidazole and citrate CDs as precursors to synthesize new functionalized CDs (FCDs) [45].

However, due to the limitations of current experimental techniques and conditions, the corrosion inhibition mechanism of heteroatom-doped CDs remains unclear. The microscopic mechanisms underlying how various improvement methods specifically enhance the performance of carbon dot corrosion inhibitors are yet to be elucidated. Carbon dot corrosion inhibitors, leveraging their high specific surface area and abundant active groups (such as pyrrole N, S, and oxygen-containing functional groups), can tightly adsorb onto the metal surface, forming a stable protective layer [17]. This protective layer not only enhances the binding force with the metal but also effectively inhibits the anode reaction, slowing down the release of metal ions and thus significantly reducing the corrosion rate. As a result, they exhibit excellent protective performance [18]. This current situation significantly restricts our ability to provide theoretical support and guidance for the in-depth design and optimization of CDs as efficient corrosion inhibitors.

In this study, we proposed the design concept of modifying charged ionic liquids at the edge of CDs to form strong adsorption sites similar to octopus brachiopods, thereby enhancing the corrosion inhibition performance of CDs ( Scheme 1). We synthesized novel N-doped CDs (NCD) as green corrosion inhibitor using ammonium citrate and urea, and adopted imidazole ionic liquids to modify the edges of NCD providing strong electrostatic adsorption force to metal surface in strong acidic solution. We found that the prepared ionic liquid-modified NCD (IL-NCD) have excellent water solubility, leading to an effective reduction of the agglomeration of CDs. The inhibition efficiency of the prepared IL-NCD as corrosion inhibitors has been thoroughly explored by weight loss and electrochemical testing techniques. The binding pattern of IL-NCD on the N80 surface was investigated by systematic experimental and molecular simulations. We have further elucidated how N-doping and ionic liquid modification enhance the performance of carbon dot corrosion inhibitors in strong acidic environment, revealing the underlying mechanisms at play. Our findings highlight the important guiding significance for the design of carbon dot corrosion inhibitors for metal protection in strong acidic environment.

All reagents and solvents were used directly without any further purification. Ammonium citrate, urea, N-ethylpiperazine, 1-bromohexane, N-(3-dimethyl laminopropyl) –N '-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride, and triethylamine were provided by Aladdin in analytical grade. N80 steel was purchased from Shandong Yangxin County Shengxin Technology Co., Ltd.

To synthesize NCD, 0.6 g ammonium citrate and 1.2 g carbamide were firstly dissolved in 10 ml water within a 50 ml beaker. Then, the reaction was carried out in a microwave oven at 80 W power for 5 min. The reaction product was dissolved in 30 ml water and centrifuged at 8000 r/min for 10 min for 3 times. The product was further purified by dialysis treatment (number average molecular weight: 500 DA) in distilled water (changed every 3 h) for 24 h, and then filtered through a PVDF membrane. Finally, the obtained solids (NCD) were freeze-dried until the moisture disappeared.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of ionic liquid-modified carbon dots (IL-NCD)
ce:section.ce:para: Efficient synthesis of IL-NCD was achieved through condensation reactions between amino groups in the ionic liquid and carboxyl groups on the surface of NCD. The specific synthesis steps are as follows. Weigh 50 mg of NCD and 1 g of 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC·HCl), and dissolve them in 100 ml of deionized water. Place the solution on a magnetic stirrer and stir at room temperature for 30 minutes to ensure full activation of the carboxyl groups on the NCD surface. Weigh 1 g of piperazine-functionalized ionic liquid and dissolve it in 20 ml of anhydrous ethanol. Slowly add 0.5 ml of triethylamine (TEA) to the solution and stir at room temperature for 10 minutes to ensure a uniform and stable solution. Gradually add the activated NCD solution dropwise to the ionic liquid solution while maintaining mechanical stirring. Continuously stir the mixed solution at room temperature for 24 hours to ensure full condensation between the carboxyl groups on the NCD surface and the amino groups in the ionic liquid. After the reaction, filter the reaction mixture using a 0.22 µm pore size polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) membrane to remove unreacted solid impurities. Transfer the filtrate to a dialysis bag with a molecular weight cutoff of 500 Da and dialyze it in deionized water for 48 hours, changing the dialysis solution every 6 hours until the solution becomes clear to thoroughly remove unreacted ionic liquid and other small molecular impurities. Dry the dialyzed product solution in a vacuum oven at 60°C for 24 hours until all solvents are completely removed. Store the dried IL-NCD product in a dry, light-protected environment for subsequent use.

The corrosion inhibition performances of NCD and IL-NCD on N80 steel were determined by the weight loss method according to GB/T 18175–2014. Firstly, all N80 steel samples (30 mm × 15 mm × 3 mm) were polished using 800#, 1500#, and 2000# metallographic sandpaper. The rust-preventive oil on the specimens was wiped off with filter paper, and then the specimens were cleaned with degreasing cotton in acetone and anhydrous ethanol, respectively. After being dried with filter paper, they were placed in a desiccator for 4 hours, weighed (accurate to 0.2 mg), and stored in the desiccator for later use. Subsequently, these samples were added to 1 M HCl solutions containing various concentrations of NCD or IL-NCD. Unless otherwise specified, the immersion test would last for 24 hours. After the test, the specimens were brushed clean with a brush, then washed in an acid pickling solution (prepared according to GB/T 18175–2014) for about 5 minutes. They were then quickly rinsed with distilled water, immediately immersed in a sodium hydroxide solution for about 30 seconds, removed, rinsed with water, wiped and dried with filter paper, soaked in anhydrous ethanol for about 3 minutes, placed on clean filter paper, dried with filter paper, and finally placed in a desiccator for more than 4 hours before being weighed (accurate to 0.2 mg).

The structures of NCD and IL-NCD were characterized by Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, NICOLET 6700) and ultraviolet-visible adsorption spectroscopy (UV–vis, Lambda 950). The bonding status and chemical composition of NCD and IL-NCD were characterized by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, AMICUS). The morphologies of NCD and IL-NCD were characterized by transmission electron microscope (TEM, TF20). After weight loss tests, the morphologies of the corrosion inhibitor films were observed by scanning electron microscope (SEM, HETACHIS-4800), and the components of adsorption films were analyzed by XPS.

The electrochemical studies were accomplished using a Gamry Reference 600 electrochemical workstation equipped with a three-electrode cell system. The scanning potential area was 300 mv via open circuit potential, and the scanning rate was 0.1 mv/s. The time to scan the open circuit potential (OCP) was 1800 s. In EIS test, scan frequency ranged from 0.01 Hz to 100,000 Hz. Tafel curves were obtained through scanning the potential in a region of 300 mV around the OCP, and the scanning rate was 0.2 mV/s. The cuboid N80 steel sample with a 1.0 cm2 surface exposed to solution was the working electrode. The platinum plate was the counter electrode. The saturated calomel was the reference electrode [19].

The VMD Carbon Dot Builder has been used to construct the structure of CDs [20,21]. To enhance the computational efficiency and ensure the simulation feasibility, a single-layer molecular structure was intercepted as the research subject. The fine-tuning of the initially constructed CDs, including the incorporation of pyrrolic N, pyridinic N, and graphitic N, the modification of ionic liquids, and the design of double-layered structure, were completed in the Materials Studio software [22]. The calculation process was carried out using Gaussian software package, with B3LYP [23] hybrid density functional theory specified as the theoretical framework, complemented by the high-accuracy 6–311 G(d,p) [24] basis set to ensure the accuracy and reliability of computational results. The integration accuracy and energy calculation convergence criteria were elevated to a rigorous level of 10−12, while strategic optimization measures such as disabling variational density fitting and direct calculation of iterative Fock matrices were implemented. Comprehensive NBO (Natural Bond Orbital) analysis was conducted and the results were preserved. Upon completion of the calculations, Multiwfn [25,26] was employed for structural analysis.

We first confirm the successful synthesis of NCD and IL-NCD. The TEM images provide the morphological and size distributions of the synthesized NCD and IL-NCD ( Fig. 1a and b). The size of NCD is between 2 and 8 nm, while the size of IL-NCD is between 4 and 10 nm. The mean particle size of NCD and IL-NCD are 5.45 nm and 6.57 nm, respectively. The elemental compositions and corresponding valence states of NCD and IL-NCD were obtained by XPS (Table S1 shows the data statistics of each element content). The full spectrum of XPS (Fig. 1c) indicates that NCD contains elements of C, N and O, while IL-NCD contains extra element of Br, which is caused by the modification of piperazine ionic liquid. After ionic liquid modification, the content of N increases from 18.96 % to 24.56 %, which is beneficial to enhance the adsorption strength of IL-NCD. Fig. 1d displays the high-resolution spectra of C 1 s, in which 284.71, 285.99, and 288.22 eV correspond to the characteristic peaks of C-C/CC, C-O/C-N, and CO, respectively. Fig. 1f shows the O 1 s peaks of NCD and IL-NCD, which contain C-O/C-N and CO structures. Fig. 1e exhibits N 1 s spectra of NCD and IL-NCD, in which the contents of pyridine-like N, graphitic N, and pyrrole-like N of NCD are 23.4 %, 37.9 %, and 38.7 %, respectively, while the contents of IL-NCD after modification of ionic liquid are 21.2 %, 32.1 %, and 46.7 %. The increase of pyrrole-like N content is beneficial to the adsorption of carbon dots corrosion inhibitor [27]. These observations show that the prepared IL-NCD has relatively uniform particle size distribution and rich functional groups, proving the successful preparation of piperazine ionic liquid doped carbon dots.

The chemical structures of NCD and IL-NCD are characterized by FTIR spectra (Fig. 1g). In the infrared spectroscopy analysis, several crucial characteristic peaks are observed, located at 1182.3 cm⁻¹ , 1357.8 cm⁻¹ , and 1707.0 cm⁻¹ , respectively, which definitively correspond to the stretching vibrations of the C-OH bond, the C-N bond, and the CO bond. Additionally, the prominent peak at 3205.7 cm⁻¹ clearly indicates the presence of symmetric stretching vibrations of the -NH₂ group. The peak at 1287.2 cm⁻¹ further confirms the stretching vibration characteristic of the C-N bond. Furthermore, the peaks at 1623.2 cm⁻¹ and 3419.8 cm⁻¹ correspond to the characteristic vibrations of the -CO-NH- group and N-H bonds, respectively, providing essential information for molecular structural interpretation [28]. Fig. 1h shows the UV–vis spectra of NCD and IL-NCD. The absorption peaks at 210 nm and 350 nm are attributed to the π-π* electronic transition of the sp2 domain on CC and n-π* electronic transition on CN, respectively [29].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical study of corrosion inhibition performance and mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: To gain a deeper understanding of the corrosion protection mechanism at the interface between N80 steel and HCl solution, and to accurately assess the durability of the protective film formed on the surface of N80 steel, we conducted a detailed study using Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS) technique. Fig. 2 presents the EIS responses of N80 steel in 1 M HCl solutions containing different concentrations (10, 20, 30, 40, and 50 mg/L) of corrosion inhibitors. As observed from the figure, both the Nyquist and Bode plots exhibit similar patterns (as shown in Fig. 2), strongly suggesting that the corrosion mechanism of N80 steel remains unchanged with increasing concentrations of specific inhibitors (such as NCD and IL-NCD) in the hydrochloric acid environment [3]. Specifically, as the concentration of the inhibitor gradually increases, the height of the capacitance arc significantly increases, which directly indicates a positive correlation between the inhibitor concentration and the inhibition effect. This positive correlation is further linked to the inhibitors protective layer formed on the surface of N80 steel, meaning that as the inhibitor concentration increases, the protective layer becomes denser, thereby providing more effective corrosion protection [4]. Furthermore, these EIS charts generally reveal that the corrosion process of N80 steel in 1.0 M HCl solution is primarily governed by the charge transfer step [4]. It is worth noting that due to the inherent roughness of the N80 steel surface and the adsorption process of inhibitors molecules, we detected a significant dispersion effect in the semicircle curve of the Nyquist plot. This dispersion effect may be the result of the combined effects of surface unevenness and the dynamic nature of the adsorption layer, which jointly influence the efficiency and path of the charge transfer process [3,4].

The electrochemical impedance spectrum is described by equivalent circuit model, and the solution resistance (R
s ), charge transfer resistance (R
ct ), double-layer capacitance (C
dl ) and corrosion inhibition efficiency (%) can be obtained by fitting with EIS analyzer program [30]. Considering that the metal surface is not ideal (uneven), the capacitance of the current circuit model must be replaced by a constant phase element (CPE), and the calculation method of CPE is given. CPE consists of two parameters, namely, admittance (Y
0 ) and exponent (n). The exponential parameter n is used to estimate the surface inhomogeneity from surface roughness, inhibitor adsorption capacity and pore layer formation. The value of n ranges from 1 to 0 and is related to the deviation of ideal capacitance behavior [31].
CPE =
Y
0
− 1
( jw )
n − 1

From the impedance parameters calculated in Table S2, it can be seen that the value of n is about 0.8, indicating the effectiveness of the designed circuit. In Nyquist diagram, the semicircular ring is not perfect, which may also be related to the micro-roughness and surface unevenness on the working electrode. Compared with the blank group, after adding 50 mg/L NCD, the R
ct value increased from 40.03 Ω‧cm2 to 823.9 Ω‧cm2. The higher R
ct value indicated that the mass and electron transfer between metal electrolytes became difficult, thus reducing the corrosion of low-carbon steel, and the corrosion inhibition efficiency decreased with increasing concentration, and the highest corrosion inhibition efficiency was 95.14 %. For IL-NCD, the corrosion inhibition efficiency is basically unchanged when the concentration is more than 40 mg/L, and the R
ct value is 973.8 Ω‧cm2and the corrosion inhibition efficiency is 95.89 %. The diameter of the capacitor ring increases with the increase of inhibitor concentration, which indicates that the inhibitor molecules are adsorbed on the metal surface. With the increase of inhibitor concentration, the coverage and adsorption capacity of the inhibitor film gradually increase, so its corrosion inhibition effect is more excellent.

We next analyzed the polarization curves with the addition of 10, 20, 30, 40, and 50 mg/L of corrosion inhibitors to 1 M HCl solutions. After extrapolating the anode and cathode branches of the measured polarization curve, such as corrosion current density (I
corr ) and corrosion potential (E
corr ), corrosion inhibition efficiency (η), anode and cathode Tafel slopes (β
a , β
c ) can be obtained by classical extrapolation method (Table S3). With the increase of inhibitor concentration, the I
corr decreases gradually. When the concentration of NCD is 50 mg/L, the current density decreases from 450.4 μA/cm2 to 11.54 μA/cm2. After adding IL-NCD, the current density decreases to 10.61 μA/cm2. The best corrosion inhibition efficiencies of NCD and IL-NCD are 97.44 % and 97.64 %, respectively, superior to other reported carbon dot corrosion inhibitors (Table S4). In addition, the E
corr of the two inhibitors move to the cathode. When the concentration of NCD and IL-NCD are 40 mg/L and 100 mg/L, the self-corrosion potential are −0.409 V and −0.394 V, respectively, which is the most obvious shift compared with the −0.426 V of the blank solution (with a shift of 17 mV and 30 mV, but less than 85 mV). This data shows that this inhibitor slows down corrosion by forming a protective film on the metal surface. On the other hand, compared with the blank solution, the cathode branch of the polarization curve containing corrosion inhibitor moves to a lower current density, while the anode branch hardly changes. The cathodic reaction is the main limitation after the addition of inhibitors. In particular, the polarization curves of all corrosion inhibitors are parallel to those in the blank solution, which shows that the mechanisms of anodic reaction (metal dissolution) and cathodic reaction (oxygen reduction) are not affected by the addition of corrosion inhibitors. Therefore, the inhibition is mainly due to the decrease of current density.

The corrosion inhibition performances of NCD and IL-NCD are better revealed by long-duration weight loss tests. The evolutions of N80 steel corrosion rate in 1 M HCl and corrosion inhibition efficiency, versus inhibitor concentration and immersion time, have been depicted ( Fig. 3). The corrosion rate of steel in blank HCl solution is the highest, and the corrosion rate decreases slightly after adding NCD. However, in IL-NCD solution, the corrosion rate of steel is one order of magnitude lower than that of blank HCl and NCD solution. In addition, the corrosion rate of steel in HCl solution fluctuates greatly with the extension of soaking time. After adding NCD, the corrosion rate of steel sheet fluctuated slightly, but gradually increased as a whole. However, the corrosion rate of steel sheet after adding IL-NCD corrosion inhibitor decreased slowly with the extension of time, showing excellent anti-corrosion effect on steel.

As a function of concentration (Fig. 3a and b), the NCD corrosion inhibitor exhibits optimal performance at a concentration of 50 mg/L, achieving a maximum inhibition efficiency of 76.32 %. In contrast, the inhibition efficiency of the IL-NCD corrosion inhibitor achieves 94.60 % at 20 mg/L, and increases slightly with increasing concentration and exhibits the largest value of approximately 97.34 % at 60 mg/L. To further verify the long-term protective performance of these two inhibitors, time-dependent tests were conducted at a concentration of 50 mg/L, with monitoring of the changes in corrosion rate and inhibition efficiency (Fig. 3c and d). In the untreated control group, the corrosion rate of the metal gradually decreases over time, attributed to the accumulation of corrosion products that formed a physical barrier to some extent against corrosive media. However, for samples treated with NCD, the corrosion rate increases over time, revealing limitations in its long-term protective capability. Excitingly, steel samples treated with IL-NCD exhibit extremely low and stable corrosion rates over long testing duration (20 d), providing compelling evidence of IL-NCD's superior performance in long-term corrosion inhibition.

Based on the corrosion rate and corrosion inhibition efficiency data of N80 steel at different temperatures shown in Fig. S1, the following analysis is drawn. Temperature has a significant effect on corrosion rate, with the corrosion rate of the blank group increasing sharply from 18.39 mm/a at 30℃ to 182.38 mm/a at 80℃, representing a nearly tenfold increase. This indicates that high temperatures significantly accelerate metal corrosion, possibly due to increased electrochemical reaction rates, enhanced solution ion activity, and decreased stability of the passive film. In terms of corrosion inhibition performance, at 30℃, NCD reduces the corrosion rate to 4.35 mm/a (corrosion inhibition efficiency of 76.32 %), while IL-NCD performs even better, with a corrosion rate of only 0.54 mm/a (corrosion inhibition efficiency of 97.04 %), approaching complete protection. At 80℃, the corrosion inhibition efficiency of NCD drops to 60.56 %, with the corrosion rate rising to 71.93 mm/a, whereas IL-NCD maintains a high corrosion inhibition efficiency of 86.15 % (corrosion rate of 25.253 mm/a). Furthermore, the corrosion inhibition efficiency of NCD decreases by 15.76 percentage points after the temperature increase, while that of IL-NCD only decreases by 10.89 percentage points. The corrosion inhibition efficiency of IL-NCD at high temperatures is significantly higher than that of NCD, highlighting its potential application value in harsh environments. Therefore, IL-NCD significantly improves the temperature adaptability of corrosion inhibition performance through ionic liquid functionalization and maintains efficient protection even at 80℃, possibly due to the synergistic effect of IL enhancing interfacial adsorption strength and thermal stability. This result provides important reference for the development of metal protective materials in high-temperature acidic or saline environments.

High concentrations of NCD can significantly enhance the π-π interactions between molecules, which subsequently promotes the aggregation of carbon dots and reduces the number of exposed effective active adsorption sites. This aggregation phenomenon directly leads to a loosening of the carbon dot film structure, thereby weakening its adsorption capacity. Ultimately, this structural change manifests as a decrease in corrosion inhibition performance. In contrast, IL-NCD, modified with ionic liquids, exhibits electrostatic repulsion due to the presence of ionic liquids, a characteristic that endows IL-NCD with excellent water solubility. Therefore, IL-NCD is able to form a denser corrosion inhibitor film, thereby exhibiting more superior corrosion inhibition performance.

In the initial stage of the experiment, once the metal surface is exposed to a corrosive environment, it often forms several initial corrosion product films. As the experiment progresses, these corrosion product films undergo a gradual stabilization process and ultimately transform into a protective barrier on the metal surface, effectively slowing down the corrosion rate. It is worth noting that the concentration of NCD may not be sufficient to fully cover the metal surface, or its chemical compatibility with the metal surface may not be high enough, both of which can directly lead to unsatisfactory corrosion inhibition effects.

In order to study the adsorption behavior of corrosion inhibitor in hydrochloric acid system, the isothermal adsorption curve of corrosion inhibitor was obtained with C/θ as the ordinate and C as the abscissa, which illustrates the influence of corrosion inhibitor molecules with different active sites on its adsorption behavior on metal surface [32].
c
θ
=
1
K
ads
+ C

According to the fitting results at different temperatures, the slope and intercept of isothermal adsorption curve can be calculated, and the adsorption or desorption intensity K
ads value and Gibbs free energy (
∆
G
ads
) [33] between adsorbate and adsorbent can be calculated by formula. The higher the K
ads value, the higher the adsorption efficiency. The free energy of adsorption
∆
G
ads
0
and K
ads are calculated as follows:
∆
G
ads
0
= − RT ln ( 1000
K
ads
)

According to the data obtained from electrochemical experiments, the adsorption isotherm curves at different temperatures are shown in Fig. 3e and f, so as to analyze the adsorption types of corrosion inhibitors on metal surfaces. The linear correlation coefficients R
2 of Langmuir isotherm fitting of NCD and IL-NCD are 0.99303 and 0.99972, respectively, which are close to 1. Which shows that the adsorption of corrosion inhibitor molecules on the metal surface conforms to the isothermal adsorption equation, that is, the corrosion inhibitor forms a dense monolayer adsorption on the metal surface, which effectively relieves the damage of hydrochloric acid to the metal. The higher the K
ads , the more the inhibitor molecules tend to adsorb on the metal surface, and the K
ads values of NCD and IL-NCD are 213.216 L/g and 1897.317 L/g, respectively. It can be seen that when ionic liquid is grafted, the adsorption performance of IL-NCD is greatly enhanced. In addition, when
∆
G
ads
0
is in the range of −20 ∼-40 kJ/mol, it shows a mixed adsorption mechanism of chemistry and physics [34].
∆
G
ads
0
is negative, indicating that the adsorption of two carbon dot inhibitors on steel surface is spontaneous. The
∆
G
ads
0
values of NCD and IL-NCD are −30.42 kJ/mol and −35.83 kJ/mol respectively. The results show that the two corrosion inhibitors are both chemical and physical mixed adsorption mechanisms. Compared with NCD, IL-NCD is more easily adsorbed on metal surface by chemical adsorption mechanism. Modification of ionic liquids can greatly enhance their chemical adsorption properties.

Fig. 4a-c show the surface morphology of N80 steel sheet in 1 M HCl, after the steel sheet was immersed for 24 h, with and without corrosion inhibitor. It can be clearly seen from the figure that the steel sheet soaked in acid solution is seriously corroded and there are many loose corrosion products on the surface. On the contrary, after adding NCD, there is a flat corrosion inhibitor film on the surface of steel sheet, but there are large cracks in the corrosion inhibitor film. However, the corrosion inhibitor film on the steel surface with IL-NCD is smoother and denser, so IL-NCD has better corrosion inhibition effect.

Fig. 4d-h show the XPS high-resolution spectrum of N80 carbon steel before and after soaking in 1 M HCl solution with and without corrosion inhibitor for 24 h. In the Fe 2p spectrum of the blank group, it appears at 710.48 and 724.06 eV corresponding to Fe2+, at 712.05, and 725.91 eV corresponding to Fe3+, and at 718.88 and 732.39 eV corresponding to satellite peaks. Combined with the elements existing on the steel surface, it is speculated that the possible corrosion products are FeOOH, FeCO3, FeO, and Fe2O3. The O 1 s [35] spectrum has four different peaks at 529.87, 531.25, 532.21, and 533.21 eV, corresponding to FeO/Fe2O3, CO, -OH, and C-O /CO3 2- respectively. The C1s [36] spectrum has four peaks, which correspond to C-C/C-H, C-O/C-N, O-C-O, and OC at 284.11 eV, 284.81 eV, 286.12 eV, and 288.41 eV, respectively. C 1 s spectra are not used to characterize inhibitors because they contain uncertain signals of carbonaceous substances. After immersion in blank solution, elements C, O and Fe were detected on the steel surface. However, after being immersed in the carbon dots solution, a new N element was observed in the XPS full spectrum of the steel surface (Fig. 4h), indicating that the prepared carbon dots corrosion inhibitor has been successfully adsorbed on the surface of N80 steel sheet. By fitting, the N 1 s spectrum has three peaks, which are located at 398.91, 400.03, and 401.14 eV respectively. The first peak is attributed to the protonated N atom (C-N) in the carbon dots corrosion inhibitor. The second peak is related to the nitrogen coordinated with the steel surface (N-Fe) in the carbon dots corrosion inhibitor. The third peak corresponds to the nitrogen protonation (N+H-) in the carbon dots corrosion inhibitor. This shows that the existence of nitrogen on the steel surface indicates that the adsorption types of corrosion inhibitors are chemical adsorption and physical adsorption [37,38].

The research results indicate that the steel surface is effectively covered by a layer of corrosion inhibitor, among which IL-NCD exhibits superior protective performance compared to NCD. IL-NCD can form a denser protective film on the steel surface, which effectively blocks the direct contact between corrosive media and the steel, thereby significantly slowing down the corrosion process. Specifically, the protective film formed by IL-NCD corrosion inhibitor performs excellently in preventing electrochemical corrosion reactions due to its high density and uniformity. This film acts as a physical barrier, reducing the erosion of the steel surface by corrosive substances such as water and oxygen, thereby extending the service life of the metallic material. Furthermore, there may be strong adsorption or chemical bonding between the IL-NCD corrosion inhibitor and the steel surface, which further enhances the stability and durability of the film. This strong binding force ensures the long-term effectiveness of the corrosion inhibitor film in practical applications, providing a reliable guarantee for the corrosion protection of metallic materials.

Fig. 4i compares the zeta potentials of NCD and IL-NCD in acidic solution. The results show that the zeta potential of NCD is −17.1 mV, indicating that its surface is negatively charged. However, due to insufficient electrostatic repulsion, particles tend to aggregate in solution, which affects their performance in corrosion inhibition applications. In contrast, the zeta potential of IL-NCD is + 56.8 mV, exhibiting a significant positive charge characteristic. This result indicates that ionic liquid modification significantly enhances the surface charge density of IL-NCD, thereby greatly improving its dispersibility in solution and effectively inhibiting particle aggregation. This improved dispersibility contributes to the formation of a more uniform and dense corrosion inhibitor film, thereby enhancing corrosion inhibition performance. The successful modulation of the surface charge characteristics of nitrogen-doped carbon dots through ionic liquid modification enables them to exhibit significantly enhanced dispersibility and corrosion inhibition performance in acidic environments. This innovation not only provides an effective strategy for addressing the issue of easy aggregation of carbon dots in corrosion inhibition applications but also offers new ideas for the development of high-performance corrosion inhibitor materials.

SEM/EDS and AFM were used as surface analysis techniques to analyze the corrosion morphology of metal surface. The SEM image of removing corrosion products on the steel surface is shown in Fig. 5. It can be clearly seen that the steel sheet in the blank solution soaked in acid solution is seriously corroded and the surface of the steel sheet is uneven. After adding NCD, the surface is relatively flat, but the pitting pits have been connected into pieces. After adding IL-NCD, the surface is smoother, and there are only a few pits on the surface, and the polished scratches are still visible. According to the elemental contents on the surfaces of the three kinds of steel sheets, the content of Fe increased from 75.82 a.t.% to 77.69 a.t.% and 80.55 a.t.% respectively, and the content of O decreased from 8.15 a.t.% to 6.78 a.t.% and 6.12 a.t.%, indicating that the corrosion products on the steel surfaces decreased. Combined with the morphology of corrosion inhibitor film, it shows that IL-NCD can form a denser protective film and has a stronger anti-corrosion effect, and the results are consistent with the weight loss data.

Atomic force microscope (AFM) was used to study the surface roughness of low carbon steel after 24 h immersion in the presence and absence of corrosion inhibitor. Fig. 5a-c are three-dimensional surface profiles of average roughness parameter R
a . The surface of blank low carbon steel shows the highest height profile, and the R
a is 223 nm, which indicates that the corrosion is serious. The existence of different corrosion inhibitors reduces the R
a of metals, and the steel surface with NCD has a smaller Ra value (207 nm>68.6 nm) than that with IL-NCD. This is because the denser corrosion inhibitor film formed by IL-NCD can effectively prevent corrosive ions from eroding metals. The observed relative surface roughness with and without inhibitors is consistent with the SEM surface morphology.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Molecular activity analysis of NCD and IL-NCD
ce:section.ce:para: In order to study the effects of N doping and ionic liquid modification on the properties of carbon dots, the molecular activities of the following models, named CD, NCD, and IL-NCD, have been calculated. Fig. 6a shows the electrostatic potential distribution and its extreme values of three molecules. The maximum electrostatic potential in CD is 9.76 kcal/mol, and the maximum and minimum point is −4.99 kcal/mol. In NCD, the negative distribution [39,40] of electrostatic potential is mainly concentrated on pyrrole N side, with the minimum value of −6.83 kcal/mol and the maximum value of 9.63 kcal/mol. N doping changes the electrostatic potential distribution of carbon dots and increases the number of electrostatic potential extremes. Pyrrole N can effectively reduce the minimum electrostatic potential of carbon dots and make a great contribution to the negative electrostatic potential. In IL-NCD, the introduction of ionic liquids induces significant alterations in the electrostatic potential distribution across the material surface. The surface electrostatic potential predominantly exhibits positive values, with a maximum value reaching 497 kcal/mol and a minimum value of −8.69 kcal/mol. Compared to CD and NCD, IL-NCD demonstrate a markedly expanded electrostatic potential range, particularly characterized by a substantial reduction in the minimum potential. This observation indicates that ionic liquid functionalization effectively enhances the electronic activity of carbon active sites. This phenomenon may be attributed to interactions between the cationic and anionic components of the ionic liquid and the carbon surface, which increase charge distribution heterogeneity and catalytic reactivity. These modifications suggest promising potential for advancing functional applications in surface adsorption.

Fig. 6b depicts the average local ionization energy (ALIE) [41] distribution profiles of three different molecules (CD, NCD, and IL-NCD), a critical parameter for evaluating the potential reactivity and reactive sites of molecules in electrophilic reactions. The blue highlighted regions in the figure intuitively point out the areas on the molecules where electrophilic reactions are most likely to occur, providing direct evidence for understanding the chemical properties of these molecules. The ALIE distribution of CD exhibits a relatively uniform characteristic, indicating a consistent responsiveness across its various parts in electrophilic reactions. In contrast, the ALIE extrema of NCD clearly cluster towards the pyrrole N atoms, with a significant increase in their number, suggesting a prominent role of pyrrole N as the active center for electrophilic reactions in NCD. Furthermore, IL-NCD, modified with ionic liquids, exhibit an even greater number of ALIE extrema, indicating the activation of additional reactive sites, which may enhance their flexibility and diversity in electrophilic reactions. However, it is noteworthy that there is an inverse relationship between the magnitude of ALIE values and electrophilic activity. Specifically, regions with higher ALIE values tend to have lower electrophilic activity. Based on this observation, it can be inferred that NCD, with their higher density and more concentrated distribution of ALIE extrema, exhibit stronger activity in electrophilic reactions compared to CD. While IL-NCD increase the number of active sites through ionic liquid modification, the electrophilic activity of these new sites is somewhat reduced due to the positive charge effect on their surface. Fig. 6c delves into the local electron attachment energy (LEAE) [42,43] and its extrema of three types of molecules to evaluate their nucleophilic reactivity potential. The blue regions clearly identify areas prone to nucleophilic attacks. Lower LEAE values indicate the presence of lower-energy empty orbitals in these regions, imparting a stronger electrophilicity, i.e., a tendency to bind with negatively charged species, and also suggesting vulnerability to nucleophilic assaults. Consequently, the nucleophilic activity of NCD is observed to be enhanced compared to CD. In addition, IL-NCD exhibit a particularly significant enhancement in the number of nucleophilic sites and the overall nucleophilic activity, which are mainly attributed to the positive charge effect introduced by the ionic liquid modification strategy. These positive charges not only enrich the surface chemical properties of carbon dots, but also serve as crucial nucleophilic active sites, greatly promoting the progress of nucleophilic reactions. It is worth emphasizing that N-doping, as an innovative chemical modification method, has a dual impact on carbon dots: on the one hand, it effectively enhances the nucleophilic activity of carbon dots, enabling these nanomaterials to exhibit higher reactivity and selectivity in reactions involving nucleophilic reagents; on the other hand, N-doping unexpectedly enhances the electrophilic activity of carbon dots. In this study, the ionic liquid not only successfully combines with N-doped carbon dots to form a stable composite structure, but more importantly, the positive charges it carries act as efficient nucleophilic active sites, significantly improving the nucleophilic activity of IL-NCD in nucleophilic reactions.

Using Fe atom as probe, the exchange repulsion potential and dispersion attraction potential of three molecules are studied, and their extreme values are extracted [39], as shown in Fig. 6d. The minimum points of NCD and CD in the three molecules are similar (0.51 kcal/mol), and the number of minimum points of IL-NCD is more than that of the other two molecules, and the value is −1.00 kcal/mol, indicating that IL-NCD has stronger interaction with Fe and is more prone to adsorption.

The AIM (Atoms-In-Molecules) paths and electron densities of carbon dots with three structures are shown in Fig. 7a, and it can be found that there are weak interactions among the three molecules. However, NCD has a smaller electron density than CD, and the electron density between IL-CD is one order of magnitude smaller, which indicates that the π-π interaction between IL-NCD layers is weaker. The π localized orbitals of three double-layered carbon dots molecules were extracted, and their morphologies were shown in Fig. 7b. It can be found that all three molecules have relatively complete π electron orbitals, which will cause π-π stacking among their molecules. It can be found that the π electron orbital of N atom in NCD is missing, which indicates that N atom can weaken π-π interaction between carbon dots to some extent.

Fig. 7c is a schematic diagram of van der Waals force between carbon dots dimers varying with their distance [44]. Compared with CD, the maximum attraction between NCD is smaller, which indicates that N doping can effectively improve the mutual attraction between carbon dots. Similarly, the attraction between IL-NCD is smaller than CD and larger than NCD. However, the distance between dimers is much larger than that between NCD and CD when its attraction is maximum. Therefore, N can reduce the accumulation of carbon dots by reducing the van der Waals interaction between dimers, while ionic liquid can increase the distance between carbon dots and reduce its accumulation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of action of NCD and IL-NCD as corrosion inhibitors
ce:section.ce:para: The mechanism of NCD corrosion inhibitor is intricate, with its core lying in the collaborative construction of a protective layer on the metal surface through the physical adsorption process mediated by van der Waals forces and the chemical adsorption mechanism involving N atoms. Specifically, van der Waals forces, as weak intermolecular forces, facilitate the initial attachment of inhibitor molecules to the metal substrate. Meanwhile, N atoms contribute by forming stable coordination bonds with the empty orbitals on the metal surface through their lone pairs of electrons, thereby deepening this attachment process and establishing a preliminary protective barrier. However, while the introduction of N atoms aims to reduce the interaction forces between carbon dots and partially perturb their π-electron systems, it fails to fully overcome the aggregation phenomenon stemming from the strong zeta potentials between carbon dots, resulting in a less dense corrosion inhibitor film and thus limited protection effectiveness.

Addressing these shortcomings, the innovation of IL-NCD corrosion inhibitor emerges through the incorporation of ionic liquids. This strategy profoundly alters the surface properties and behavioral patterns of carbon dots. Firstly, the attachment of ionic liquids endows carbon dots with abundant positive charges, leveraging electrostatic repulsion to effectively weaken the interaction forces between carbon dots, manifested by a notable increase in zeta potential values, which significantly mitigates their tendency to aggregate. Secondly, the introduction of ionic liquids not only enhances the surface activity of carbon dots but also significantly increases the active sites available for interaction with the metal surface, particularly the augmentation of nucleophilic active sites, thereby bolstering the construction of a denser and more stable corrosion inhibitor film by IL-NCD on the metal surface. This film not only isolates corrosive media more effectively but also demonstrates superior anti-corrosion properties ( Fig. 8).

In summary, the imidazolium ionic liquid-modified CDs corrosion inhibitor has been successfully prepared, and the weight loss experiment shows that its corrosion inhibition efficiency reaches 97.34 % at 60 mg/L, exceeding 92.08 % of the unmodified CDs in 1 M HCl. The NCD corrosion inhibition mechanism is complex, combining van der Waals force physical adsorption with N-atom chemical adsorption to construct a protective layer on the metal surface. Van der Waals force initially adheres to the metal, and the lone pair electrons of the N atom coordinate with the metal empty orbitals to deepen the attachment, but the N atom fails to completely overcome the agglomeration of carbon dots, affecting the compactness of the film. The ionic liquid endows the carbon dots with positive charges, reducing agglomeration through electrostatic repulsion and improving the zeta potential. Meanwhile, it enhances surface activity and nucleophilic sites, promoting close bonding with the metal to form a denser and more stable protective film, effectively isolating corrosive media. In conclusion, IL-NCD achieves efficient and long-lasting protection of metals through innovative adsorption mechanisms and surface optimization. Further research is needed to explore the specific interactions of carbon dots with various metal surfaces and synergistic effects with other inhibitors. Future studies should focus on enhancing their performance through functional group modification or material compositing. As green, efficient inhibitors, carbon dots offer excellent biocompatibility and environmental benefits, holding great potential for low-carbon applications. Nonetheless, challenges such as insufficient stability and high costs in practical use remain critical issues for future research to address.

Fu Yu: Validation, Software, Funding acquisition. Hu Songqing: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Wang Zhikun: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Wang Jingyue: Validation, Software. Peng Yunchao: Software, Methodology. Li Fengting: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Qu Wentao: Software, Methodology. Su Huiling: Data curation, Conceptualization. Li Miantuo: Methodology, Investigation. Yang Liqiang: Methodology, Investigation. Wang Li: Methodology, Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (52204066, 52474020), the Shandong Province Youth Entrepreneurship Technology Support Program for Higher Education Institutions (2023KJ060), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (23CX06018A), and Shandong Provincial Natural Science Foundation, China (ZR2024QE109).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.corsci.2025.112798.
